hub_subtitle: Conversação para Feiras Internacionais de Turismo & Hotelaria

lesson.menu_desc: Networking language, follow-ups and long-term relationships -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 6 -- Making It Stick
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Andrea Aggio | Aula 6 -- Making It Stick

listenings.text: Hi Andrea, it is Paulo. It was a real pleasure to meet you at the fair last week. I just wanted to follow up and keep you posted on our conversation. My team loved your itineraries, and we would like to move forward. As promised, I could send you our full rate sheet and organize a short product presentation whenever it suits you. I really think there is a mutual benefit here, and we would love to make Escape Turismo a preferred partner in Portugal. If you agree, we should also talk about a room block and a possible volume agreement for next season. Let us stay in touch. Please call me back this week, and we will take the next step together.

Good afternoon, and thank you for joining our partner webinar. Today we would like to explain how our new preferred partner program works. First, every partner receives an updated rate sheet and access to exclusive deals that are not available to the public. Second, if you book a large volume, we can offer a special volume agreement with better commissions. We strongly recommend that you keep our team posted about your upcoming groups, because we could reserve a room block in advance and guarantee availability. Building a long-term relationship is our main goal, so please stay in touch, and do not hesitate to circle back with any questions. We look forward to growing together.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: to keep contact with someone over time
an offer only one partner receives
to return to a topic or contact someone again later
a group of rooms reserved for one client or event
a document listing a hotel's prices
to keep someone informed of news
a company you work with first, by agreement

paras: At every fair, thousands of business cards change hands. Most of them end up forgotten in a drawer. The best professionals know a simple truth: the real work does not happen at the fair -- it happens in the days that follow. Meeting someone is easy. Making the relationship stick is the hard, valuable part.

The secret is the follow-up. A great networker sends a short message within forty-eight hours. 'It was a pleasure to meet you. Could we schedule a quick call?' They keep the other person posted, they circle back when they say they will, and they always look for a mutual benefit, not just a quick sale. Politeness matters, too. 'Could you send me your rate sheet?' sounds far better than a blunt demand, and 'I would love to work with you' opens more doors than 'I want a deal'.

Over time, these small habits build something powerful: a long-term relationship. A supplier who trusts you becomes a preferred partner. A preferred partner offers you exclusive deals and better rates. In this industry, your network is your true portfolio -- and the professionals who make it stick are the ones who succeed.
reading.source: Adapted from a networking guide, The Relationship Advantage

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Collecting as many business cards as possible at a fair is what makes you successful.
The real value comes after the fair -- polite follow-ups turn contacts into long-term partners.
Great networkers avoid following up because it looks too eager.

items: According to the text, most business cards end up forgotten.
The first paragraph says most of them end up forgotten in a drawer.
The text says the real work happens during the fair, not after it.
It says the real work happens in the days that follow, not at the fair.
A great networker follows up within forty-eight hours.
The second paragraph states this directly.
The text says being blunt and demanding works better than being polite.
It says polite language opens more doors than blunt demands.

gapfill.text: Notice the polite modals: 'Could you send me...?' is softer than 'Send me...', and 'I would love to...' is warmer than 'I want...'. After could, would, and should, we always use the base verb (no 'to'): 'You should visit', not 'You should to visit'.

lf.title: Request, offer, or recommendation?
items: you send me your rate sheet?
love to schedule a product presentation.
visit our destination in high season.
reserve a room block in advance.

lf.text: Notice: 'could' makes a polite request or a soft suggestion, 'would' makes a warm offer, and 'should' gives a recommendation. All three are followed by the base verb: could send, would love, should visit -- never 'to'.

bank.label: Useful language for follow-ups
items: It was a pleasure to meet you.
Could you send me your rate sheet?
I would love to schedule a call.
We should talk about a volume agreement.
I will keep you posted on the dates.
I will circle back next week.
I look forward to a long-term relationship.
Write a follow-up message to a supplier you met at the fair. Make a polite request with 'could' and a warm offer with 'would'.
A partner asks for advice on when to visit your destination. Give a recommendation using 'should'.
Explain the mutual benefit of becoming a preferred partner, using 'could', 'would', and 'should' at least once each.
Close a conversation warmly: offer to stay in touch, keep them posted, and circle back with the details.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = stay, 2 = keep, 3 = mutual benefit, 4 = preferred partner, 5 = exclusive deal.
Model request (could): 'Could you send me your rate sheet before Friday?'
Model offer (would): 'I would love to give you a short product presentation next week.'
Model recommendation (should): 'You should visit our destination in high season -- it is the best time.'
answerkey.note: After could, would, and should, always use the base verb, with no 'to': could send, would love, should visit.
